title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigating potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand vehicle intentions at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
domain: Human-Computer Interaction, Autonomous Vehicles, Transportation Research
method_summary: VR experiment with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Used a 4x4 randomized complete block design testing 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' text, flashing smile, control) and 4 audible features (horn, music, verbal message, control). Collected objective measures (crossing time, waiting time from headset tracking) and subjective measures (feature ratings, receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale).
method_family: Virtual Reality Experiment, Randomized Complete Block Design
datasets: Virtual environment developed using Unity 3D with HTC Vive VR headset

main_claims.claim_text: Inclusion of external features significantly increased pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who frequently commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAVs poorly even with external features
VR setting works well as a valuable mode for research involving FAVs and pedestrians

limitations.text: Study used a non-signalized intersection with single controlled scenario where FAV approached from one direction and stopped at intersection
No other traffic was included in the virtual environment to eliminate distractions, reducing ecological validity
Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in every trial - did not study feature termination or negative signals
Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability of findings
Participants were primarily from US and all fluent English speakers; findings may not generalize to other cultures or language groups

future_work: Investigate complex traffic systems with signals, presence of vehicle operator, and pedestrian signals at crosswalk
Include near-miss conditions to observe pedestrian perception of highly rated features
Study start and termination of signals at intersections
Examine feasibility and preference for reported features from other road-users' perspectives
Test positive and negative interface communication signals
Add environmental sounds and distractions to increase realism
notes: Validated VR platform for pedestrian safety research (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017). First-time exposure for all participants to FAVs. Walking silhouette considered familiar as it mimics existing pedestrian crossing signals. Verbal message beneficial for visually impaired pedestrians and those distracted. Smiley face confused participants about vehicle's intended action.
